confirmed_findings.title: MLE chi fit test uses a physically inconsistent fixture (no FP07 rolloff applied, but H2 handed to the fitter), never asserts chi (7.9x true on its own fixture), and misattributes its factor-2 kB tolerance to 'grid search'
confirmed_findings.claim: TestMLEFit.test_synthetic_recovery (tests/test_chi.py:461-504) builds spec_obs = batchelor_grad(K, kB_true, chi_true) WITHOUT multiplying by H2 (:479-480) yet passes H2 to _mle_fit_kB (:493), so the fitter corrects for an attenuation that never happened: on the test's own fixture returned chi is 7.9x chi_true and kB 17% high (62% at eps=1e-8) — the test discards _chi_fit entirely (:487) and its 0.5<kB ratio<2.0 window exists only to absorb the fixture bias, while the docstring blames 'broader tolerance for grid search'.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Measured at HEAD: test fixture gives kB ratio 1.173, chi ratio 7.94 (eps=1e-7); at eps=1e-8 kB ratio 1.619 (near the 2.0 limit), chi ratio 6.39. With a consistent fixture (batchelor_grad*H2 + noise_K) the same function recovers kB 0.996-1.003, chi 0.994-1.003, eps 0.982-1.012 across eps 1e-9..1e-7 — the fit is 0.5%-accurate. This is the only synthetic-accuracy test of the user-selectable fit_method='mle' chi path (rsi/config.py:116); a genuine order-of-magnitude chi error there would pass (the real-data cross-check at tests/test_chi.py:1928-1942 compares only epsilon_T).
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: PYTHONPATH=... python3 scratchpad/mle_consistent.py — '[test fixture] eps=1e-07 kB ratio=1.173 chi ratio=7.943' vs 'kB ratio=0.996 chi ratio=0.994' for the consistent fixture. Compare tests/test_chi.py:430-434 (Method-1 test applies H2) with :479-480.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Make the fixture consistent (spec_obs = maximum(batchelor_grad*H2 + noise_K, 1e-20)), tighten to ~5% on kB, add chi_fit/eps_fit assertions (rtol~0.05), delete the misleading 'grid search' comment.

verdict.reasoning: CONFIRMED & reproduced. tests/test_chi.py:479 builds spec_obs=batchelor_grad(K,kB,chi) with NO H2, yet :493 passes H2 to _mle_fit_kB, which de-attenuates variance that was never applied (chi.py:445-456 correction). Ran fixture: kB ratio 1.173 / chi ratio 7.94 at eps=1e-7; kB 1.619 at eps=1e-8 (near the 2.0 assert limit). A consistent fixture (batchelor_grad*H2+noise) recovers kB/chi/eps to 0.5-2%. The test discards _chi_fit (:487), asserts only 0.5<kB<2.0, and its docstring blames 'grid search' rather than the fixture bias. Contrast Method-1 test :434 which correctly applies H2. fit_method='mle' is user-selectable (config.py:53,116). Production _mle_fit_kB is correct; this is only a coverage/test-quality gap + misleading docstring, so minor.

confirmed_findings.title: docs/DATA_FLOW.md still claims L5 depth-binning uses a geometric mean for epsilon/chi — stale since PR #68 switched the rsi pipeline to arithmetic binning
confirmed_findings.claim: DATA_FLOW.md's L5 section states 'Log-normal variables (epsilon, chi) use geometric mean; others use arithmetic mean', but at HEAD the rsi pipeline depth-bins epsilon and chi arithmetically (deliberately, to estimate the flux-relevant mean and match perturb), so the doc misstates the statistical meaning of the binned headline products by a systematic factor exp(sigma_ln^2/2) (~1.6-3x for typical sigma_ln 1-1.5) for anyone correcting or comparing per the doc.
confirmed_findings.evidence: docs/DATA_FLOW.md:197-199 (last touched abb1930/PR #54). rsi/binning.py:96-97 defaults log_mean_vars=None->empty ('EVERY variable is averaged arithmetically'), docstring :82-90 explains the geometric mean 'estimates the MEDIAN and biases binned dissipation low'; rsi/pipeline.py:650-656,:662-668 call bin_by_depth WITHOUT log_mean_vars. Commit 6550022 (PR #68) postdates the doc's last edit. ARCHITECTURE.md:408 already says 'arithmetic mean' — the two overview docs contradict each other.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: sed -n '196,199p' docs/DATA_FLOW.md; sed -n '96,97p;162,170p' src/odas_tpw/rsi/binning.py; grep -n 'log_mean_vars' src/odas_tpw/rsi/pipeline.py; git log --oneline -- src/odas_tpw/rsi/binning.py (6550022) vs docs/DATA_FLOW.md (abb1930).
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Update the L5 section: all variables depth-binned with the arithmetic mean; geometric averaging only via the opt-in log_mean_vars argument.

verdict.reasoning: CONFIRMED. DATA_FLOW.md:197-199 states 'Log-normal variables (epsilon, chi) use geometric mean.' Actual code: rsi/pipeline.py:650,662 call bin_by_depth WITHOUT log_mean_vars; binning.py:96-97 defaults to empty set = arithmetic ('EVERY variable averaged arithmetically'). Ran bin_by_depth: default branch is arithmetic (verified vs explicit log_mean_vars set giving a 2.34x gap). Git: binning switched to arithmetic in 6550022 (PR #68); DATA_FLOW.md last touched abb1930 (PR #54, predates), so the doc is stale. ARCHITECTURE.md:408 already says 'arithmetic mean', so the two overview docs contradict. A reader trusting DATA_FLOW.md would misinterpret the binned headline products (median vs mean, factor exp(sigma^2/2)~1.6-3x). Doc/code mismatch with scientific meaning -> minor.

confirmed_findings.title: _qc_chi_final applies the epsilon-FM threshold (1.15) one-sidedly to the two-sided chi variance-ratio fom, so severely bad fits (fom<<1) pass QC into K_T/Gamma while mildly high fits are dropped
confirmed_findings.claim: The rsi run_pipeline mixing-QC filter added for audit #32 gates the chi obs/model variance ratio with 'fom <= 1.15' only — a one-sided cut at a threshold defined for the Lueck MAD-based FM statistic — even though the package's own docs define the chi fom as bad whenever it is far from 1.0 in either direction, so a window whose model overestimates observed variance 5x (fom=0.2) passes into K_T/Gamma while a window 20% high (fom=1.2) is rejected.
confirmed_findings.evidence: rsi/pipeline.py:42 _CHI_FOM_LIMIT=1.15 with comment 'Mirror the chi-from-epsilon fom_limit in window.py' — but window.py:272-273,347-353 applies 1.15 to the EPSILON figure of merit (Lueck FM convention), not the chi variance ratio. passes_qc (:67-73) has only '(fom <= fom_limit)', no lower bound. docs/chi_mathematics.md (~:490-499) defines chi fom as 'Values near 1.0 indicate a good fit; values far from 1.0 indicate the model does not explain the observed variance', 'not the MAD-based FM'. Reproduced: _qc_chi_final with fom rows [0.20,1.20,0.15] kept fom=0.20, dropped fom=1.20, passed a fom=0.15-only window. perturb's _apply_fom_cut (perturb/pipeline.py:600) shares the one-sidedness but is opt-in; the rsi limit is hardcoded on the default run_pipeline path feeding L4/L5 K_T and Gamma. Distinct from prior #42/#101 (epsilon-side statistic swap) — this is a new defect in the post-audit #32 fix.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: PYTHONPATH=... python3 scratchpad/verify_stats.py section 1 — _qc_chi_final keeps fom=0.20, drops fom=1.20, passes fom=0.15-only. Read rsi/pipeline.py:37-73, window.py:272-273,347-353, chi_mathematics.md fom section.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Gate two-sidedly, e.g. abs(log(fom)) <= log(fom_limit) (band 1/1.15..1.15 or a validated wider band); do the same for perturb's chi _apply_fom_cut.

verdict.reasoning: CONFIRMED, reachable, not a #32/#42 duplicate (new defect in the PR#79 #32-fix). pipeline.py:67-73 `_qc_chi_final.passes_qc` applies only `fom <= 1.15` (one-sided) to the chi variance-ratio fom feeding K_T/Gamma (called :543 on default run_pipeline path). But chi_mathematics.md:498 defines the chi fom as two-sided ('values far from 1.0 in either direction... not the MAD-based FM'); the 1.15 was written for window.py's EPSILON fom (:349). Ran _qc_chi_final([0.20,1.20,0.15]): keeps fom=0.20, drops fom=1.20. Reachability: on the DEFAULT Method-1 path (chi_primary=l4_chi_eps, pipeline.py:508) real SN479 data has 13.6% of windows with fom in [0.5,0.87) that a symmetric cut would reject but the one-sided cut admits (Method-2 min fom was 0.93, so M2 alone never triggers it). Scientific impact bounded (chi anchored to obs_var, not the model; fallback-to-all-probes), so minor.

confirmed_findings.title: Perturb campaign pipeline applies no ATOMIX spectral-fit QC (FM/variance-resolved flags) to epsilon before combining and binning, despite computing and writing FM
confirmed_findings.claim: On the default ARCTERX/perturb path, per-probe epsilon is written with no flag-based masking, the only spectral-fit knob (epsilon.fom_max) defaults to null and thresholds the non-ATOMIX variance-ratio fom rather than the Lueck FM the file also carries, so epsilonMean -> depth bins -> combo include estimates the package's own ATOMIX QC (FM>1.15, var_resolved<0.5, despike limits) would reject — while the rsi run_pipeline path applies the full flag set via _compute_flags/_compute_epsi_final.
confirmed_findings.evidence: dissipation.py:265 'epsilon[ci, j] = e_4' with no flag mask; the module never references _compute_flags/_compute_epsi_final yet computes and writes FM (dissipation.py:206,269,468-481, whose own comment says 'ATOMIX recommends rejecting FM > ~1.15'). perturb/pipeline.py:1381-1387 applies only optional _apply_fom_cut (fom_max default None, config.py:106) on the variance-ratio fom; no code in perturb thresholds FM. mk_epsilon_mean's CI removal is dead for the 2-probe SN479 (n_probes>=3 gate, epsilon_combine.py:136 — prior-audit M1 territory), so the combined campaign epsilon receives zero spectral-fit QC by default. Contrast l4.py:249-264. docs/perturb/pipeline.md:47 describes only 'geometric mean with 95% CI filtering'.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: grep -n 'epsi_flags\|_compute_flags\|_compute_epsi_final' src/odas_tpw/rsi/dissipation.py (no hits); grep -n 'FM' src/odas_tpw/perturb/pipeline.py (no threshold); read perturb/pipeline.py:1381-1387, config.py:106, scor160/l4.py:246-264.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Add an FM-based per-probe cut in the perturb diss stage (FM_max config, default 1.15) before mk_epsilon_mean, or document that campaign epsilon products carry no spectral-fit QC unless fom_max is set.

verdict.reasoning: CONFIRMED. Perturb calls dissipation.py `_compute_epsilon` (pipeline.py:1364) which stores raw e_4 (dissipation.py:265) and never calls _compute_flags/_compute_epsi_final (0 grep hits); it writes FM (:481, comment 'ATOMIX recommends rejecting FM>~1.15') but nothing thresholds it. Only spectral knob epsilon.fom_max defaults None (config.py:106) and cuts the variance-ratio fom, not FM; mk_epsilon_mean does inter-probe CI only, inactive for 2-probe SN479 (epsilon_combine.py:136 n_probes>=3); qc_gate uses external hotel channels, not spectral. rsi run_pipeline instead bins process_l4's flag-masked epsi_final (pipeline.py:429,652). Ran _compute_epsilon on SN479_0006: 18% of windows FM>1.15; removing them drops the aggregate arithmetic-mean epsilon 3.85x (geo 1.18x). ARCHITECTURE.md:433-448 DOCUMENTS this as intentional but claims it is 'QC philosophy rather than science' and paths 'agree numerically for two-probe data' — contradicted by the measured 3.85x/1.18x. Documented-intentional caps it at minor, but the doc misrepresents the numeric impact -> real minor gap, not refuted.

confirmed_findings.title: pyturb-cli bin silently depth-bins epsilon/chi with the geometric mean (up to ~14x lower than the perturb/rsi arithmetic convention), undisclosed in its CLI docs
confirmed_findings.claim: pyturb-cli bin hard-codes log_mean_vars={'eps_1','eps_2','eps_final','epsilon','chi','chi_final','epsi_final'} with no CLI flag, so its binned dissipation estimates the per-bin MEDIAN (geometric mean) and is systematically lower than the arithmetic-mean binned products from perturb/rsi on the same data — by factor exp(-sigma_ln^2/2): measured 0.61x at sigma_ln=1.0, 0.32x at 1.5 (2.27x gap at 1.3, matching theory to 3 decimals), 0.07x at 2.3 — while docs/pyturb/PYTURB_CLI.md never mentions the convention.
confirmed_findings.evidence: pyturb/bin.py:40 hard-codes the set, passed at :87 ('log_mean_vars & set(var_names)'); no CLI option. rsi/binning.py:82-92 (shared engine docstring) states the package convention is arithmetic and the geometric mean 'estimates the MEDIAN and biases binned dissipation low'; geometric branch attaches a warning comment (:154-160). Reproduced: 20000 lognormal samples in one 2-m bin: geo/ari = 0.608/0.324/0.073 for sigma_ln = 1.0/1.5/2.3 (theory exp(-sigma^2/2)); bin_by_depth on 4000 samples at sigma_ln=1.3: arithmetic 2.399e-08 vs geometric 1.059e-08, ratio 2.27 (theory 2.33). PYTURB_CLI.md:92-107 documents bin flags with no mention of geometric averaging, so a user comparing against a perturb combo (or computing K_rho from binned epsilon) inherits a hidden multiplicative bias.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: PYTHONPATH=... python3 scratchpad/verify_stats2.py sections 5-6 (measured geo/ari ratios and the hard-coded set); bin_by_depth on lognormal eps with/without log_mean_vars={'eps_1'} -> ~2.3x gap; grep -n 'geometric\|log_mean' docs/pyturb/PYTURB_CLI.md (no hits).
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Add a --log-mean/--linear-mean option (or default arithmetic to match the package convention); document the median-not-mean semantics in PYTURB_CLI.md and in output attrs (cell_methods 'depth: geometric_mean'); if geometric is intentional Jesse-parity, say so explicitly.
merged_from: pyturb-cli bin geometrically averages binned epsilon, contradicting the repo's own binning doctrine and the main pipeline: binned dissipation biased low by ~2.3x

verdict.reasoning: CONFIRMED & reproduced. pyturb/bin.py:40 hard-codes log_mean_vars={eps_1,eps_2,eps_final,epsilon,chi,chi_final,epsi_final}, passed :87; cli.py:84-100 exposes no averaging flag. So pyturb-cli bin geometric-means dissipation while rsi/perturb bin arithmetically (binning.py:82-90 states arithmetic is the package convention; geometric 'estimates the MEDIAN and biases binned dissipation low'). Ran: geo/ari = 0.603/0.431/0.325/0.070 at sigma_ln=1.0/1.3/1.5/2.3 (matches exp(-s^2/2)); bin_by_depth arithmetic 2.331e-08 vs geometric 9.97e-09, ratio 2.338 (theory 2.328). PYTURB_CLI.md:92-107 documents the bin flags with no mention of geometric/log-mean/median (grep empty). Not in prior audits. Geometric mean is a legitimate oceanographic convention, so pyturb is not internally 'wrong', but the undocumented, un-overridable inconsistency with the rest of the package injects a hidden 1.6-14x factor when comparing tools or deriving K_rho -> minor (touches statistical meaning of the headline product).

confirmed_findings.title: mk_chi_mean docstring step 7 describes the OLD combine rule ('remove the largest probe value'), contradicting the implemented symmetric furthest-from-ln-mean removal
confirmed_findings.claim: The public API docstring of mk_chi_mean (step 7: 'Iteratively remove the largest probe value from rows whose min/max log-spread exceeds the CI range') documents the pre-fix always-drop-max rule, while the code (chi_combine.py:158-188) drops the probe furthest from the cross-probe ln-mean and only with >=3 probes — for a low-outlier case the two rules differ by a factor of ~346 in chiMean.
confirmed_findings.evidence: chi_combine.py:54-55 vs inline comment :158-162 ('The old code always dropped the maximum...') and implementation :163-188. Reproduced: 3 probes {1.0e-7, 1.2e-7, 1.0e-12} with epsilon_T -> chiMean = 1.0954e-07 (low outlier removed); the docstring rule would yield geomean{1.0e-7,1.0e-12} = 3.16e-10, 346x lower. The epsilon twin (epsilon_combine.py:38) was updated; only the chi docstring is stale.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: PYTHONPATH=... python3 scratchpad/verify_stats.py section 2 — chiMean=1.0954e-07 vs 3.16e-10. Read chi_combine.py:54-57 vs :158-188.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite step 7: 'Iteratively remove the probe furthest from the cross-probe ln-mean (symmetric) on rows whose log-spread exceeds the CI range; requires >= 3 probes, never prunes below 2 survivors.'

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed doc/code self-inconsistency. chi_combine.py:54-55 docstring step 7 says 'Iteratively remove the largest probe value', the pre-fix always-drop-max rule. Implementation at :158-188 drops the probe FURTHEST from the cross-probe ln-mean and only with n_probes>=3 (comment :158-162 explicitly says 'The old code always dropped the maximum'). Reproduced with {1.0e-7,1.2e-7,1.0e-12} + epsilon_T: chiMean=1.0954e-07 (low outlier 1e-12 dropped) vs the docstring rule's geomean{1e-7,1e-12}=3.162e-10, ratio=346x. Epsilon twin docstring epsilon_combine.py:38 reads generically ('remove probes outside CI'), so only the chi docstring is stale. Code is correct; the stale docstring would mislead a reader about the combine algorithm. Minor (doc/code mismatch, bounded impact) per rubric.

confirmed_findings.title: quick_look chi_method=2 computes the iterative fit but labels and highlights it as 'M2-MLE'
confirmed_findings.claim: With chi_method=2, the chi-profile panel plots iterative-fit chi (compute_chi_window(method=2) -> _iterative_fit) but titles it 'M2-MLE', and the chi-spectra panel stars/thickens the M2-MLE curve — a genuinely different fit whose chi values do not match the profile panel; the quick_look docstring says '2 = MLE Batchelor fit' while the CLI help says '2 = iterative spectral fit'.
confirmed_findings.evidence: quick_look.py:257-271 calls compute_chi_window(method=2); window.py:402-408 'Method 2: iterative fit' -> _iterative_fit — the same computation the spectra panel plots as 'M2-Iter' (quick_look.py:102-113,135-140). Yet :413 titles the profile panel {1:'M1', 2:'M2-MLE'} and :568 sets selected_method 'M2-MLE', so :592-597 draw the _mle_fit_kB curve (distinct estimator, :142-165) thick and starred. Docstring :713-714 says 'MLE Batchelor fit'; rsi/cli.py:1041-1042 says 'iterative spectral fit'.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: Read quick_look.py:257-258, 413, 568, 102-113 vs 142-165, window.py:402-415, cli.py:1037-1043. Optionally run rsi-tpw ql on 0002.p --chi-method 2 and compare the starred 'M2-MLE chi=' legend value against the chi-profile panel values.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Map chi_method=2 to 'M2-Iter' in both label dicts and fix the docstring (or switch _compute_windowed_eps_chi to the MLE path if that was intended).

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by reading all cited sites. Profile panel: chi_arr from _compute_windowed_eps_chi with chi_method=2 -> compute_chi_window(method=2) -> _iterative_fit (window.py:402-419), i.e. the M2-Iter estimator; yet quick_look.py:413 labels it 'M2-MLE'. Spectra panel: :568 selected_method='M2-MLE' and :592-597 draw the M2-MLE curve thick/starred; M2-MLE is computed via _mle_fit_kB directly (:142-165), a genuinely distinct estimator whose chi need not equal the profile-panel (iterative) value. Docstring :713-714 says '2 = MLE Batchelor fit' but cli.py:1041-1042 and window.py:402 say '2 = iterative spectral fit'. Real self-inconsistency, but confined to the rsi-tpw ql diagnostic/QC plot, not production epsilon/chi numbers. Minor.

confirmed_findings.title: parse_time rejects valid unquoted UTC 'Z' timestamps in sections.yaml and figure specs
confirmed_findings.claim: An unquoted UTC timestamp with trailing Z (e.g. start: 2025-01-20T00:00:00Z) in a sections.yaml or figure-spec inline sections block is resolved by ruamel.yaml to a tz-aware datetime whose str() is '2025-01-20 00:00:00+00:00', so parse_time's offset check sees '+00:00' and raises ValueError telling the user to 'use a trailing Z' — exactly what they wrote — making a valid UTC section window unusable unless quoted.
confirmed_findings.evidence: sections.py:104 s=str(value); ruamel rt mode (sections.py:189-191) and safe mode (figure.py:75) resolve unquoted timestamps to tz-aware TimeStamp; str() renders '+00:00', rejected at sections.py:114-120. Verified by running: YAML().load('start: 2025-01-20T00:00:00Z') -> TimeStamp(tzinfo=utc); parse_time raises. The docstring example (:178) only works because quoted. Clean error (no silent shift), bounding impact.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: PYTHONPATH=... python3 -c "from ruamel.yaml import YAML; from odas_tpw.perturb.plot.sections import parse_time; v=YAML().load('start: 2025-01-20T00:00:00Z')['start']; print(type(v)); parse_time(v)" — ValueError despite UTC value.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Handle datetime inputs before stringification: accept tz-aware datetimes with utcoffset()==0 (convert to naive UTC), reject only non-zero offsets, pass naive datetimes through.

verdict.reasoning: Reproduced. YAML().load('start: 2025-01-20T00:00:00Z') (rt mode, used by load_sections sections.py:190) yields ruamel TimeStamp tzinfo='Z'; str() renders '2025-01-20T00:00:00+00:00'. safe mode (figure.py) yields datetime with str() '2025-01-20 00:00:00+00:00'. parse_time strips only a literal trailing 'Z' (:107-108); neither string ends in 'Z', so the offset check (:114-120) sees '+00:00' and raises ValueError telling the user to 'use a trailing Z' -- exactly what they wrote. Quoted '"...Z"' stays a str and parses fine. Valid unquoted UTC section window is unusable unless quoted. Clean error, no silent shift, workaround exists; perturb/plot config surface. Minor.

confirmed_findings.title: pyturb-cli eps window parameters computed once at 512 Hz and never recomputed per file, contradicting its own comments; wrong window durations for non-512-Hz instruments
confirmed_findings.claim: run_eps converts --fft-len/--diss-len seconds to samples exactly once with fs_default = 512.0 (eps.py:275-278) and _process_one_file only overwrites l3_params.fs_fast (:83,:128), never the sample counts, so for any instrument not sampling at 512 Hz the physical window durations are wrong by 512/fs (e.g. '-f 1.0' gives a 0.5 s FFT at 1024 Hz) — while two inline comments claim the values 'will be overridden per-file' (:274) and 'will be overwritten below' (:73).
confirmed_findings.evidence: eps.py:273-278 (single call to compute_window_parameters with fs_default=512.0); grep confirms one call site; :73-74 comment vs only fs_fast updated at :83,:128. ARCTERX SN479 is 512 Hz so in-house data unaffected; the CLI accepts arbitrary .p/.nc input.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: Read src/odas_tpw/pyturb/eps.py:68-83, 123-128, 273-296; grep -rn compute_window_parameters src/odas_tpw/pyturb/.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Move compute_window_parameters inside _process_one_file after fs_fast is known, or delete the misleading comments and document 512-Hz-sample semantics.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed. _compat.compute_window_parameters (line 35-38) makes both counts fs-dependent: n_fft=seconds_to_samples(fft_len,fs), n_diss=round(diss_len*fs). run_eps calls it exactly once with fs_default=512.0 (eps.py:275-278) and passes fixed fft_length/diss_length via common_kwargs. _process_one_file only overwrites l3_params.fs_fast (:83,:128); sample counts never recomputed. So at 1024 Hz, '-f 1.0' still gives n_fft=512 samples = 0.5 s window (wrong duration by 512/fs). Comments :274 ('will be overridden per-file') and :73 ('will be overwritten below') falsely claim override. ARCTERX SN479 is 512 Hz so in-house data unaffected; latent for other-rate instruments the CLI accepts. Self-inconsistency, minor.

confirmed_findings.title: pyturb-cli documents --despike-passes, -t/--temperature, and --speed as functional but all three are silently ignored; despike runs 10 passes, not the documented pyturb-matching 6
confirmed_findings.claim: docs/pyturb/PYTURB_CLI.md:68-75 documents -t/--temperature, --speed, and --despike-passes (default 6) as working eps options, but eps.py never reads args.temperature or args.speed, and _process_one_file accepts despike_passes (eps.py:35, populated at :291) without using it — despike max_passes stays at the scor160 default of 10 (despike.py:41), so users setting these flags get silently different processing than requested.
confirmed_findings.evidence: inspect.getsource(_process_one_file): zero body references to 'despike_passes' or 'temperature' (only the signature); no 'args.temperature'/'args.speed' anywhere in pyturb/eps.py; L2Params (eps.py:58-66) carries no pass count; despike.py:41 max_passes=10. cli.py:53-54,62 define the flags; they are NOT marked '(not implemented)' unlike --aoa/--pitch-correction (cli.py:77-80,121-124).
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: PYTHONPATH=... python3 -c "import inspect, odas_tpw.pyturb.eps as e; print([l for l in inspect.getsource(e._process_one_file).splitlines() if 'despike_passes' in l or 'temperature' in l])" — only the signature. Compare PYTURB_CLI.md:68-75.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Thread despike_passes into despike() via L2 params and implement temperature/speed variable selection, or mark all three '(not implemented)' with a runtime warning like --aoa; fix the PYTURB_CLI.md table.

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed. PYTURB_CLI.md table documents -t/--temperature, --speed, and --despike-passes (default 6) as functional eps flags. grep/inspect: eps.py never references args.temperature or args.speed; despike_passes appears only in the signature (:35) and is plumbed from args (:291) but never used in _process_one_file's body; L2Params carries no pass count, so despike runs the scor160 default max_passes=10 (despike.py:42), not 6. cli.py:53-54,62 define the flags without a '(not implemented)' note, unlike --aoa/--pitch-correction which explicitly warn (cli.py:77,79,121-124). Users setting these get silently different processing than documented. Doc/code mismatch in the secondary pyturb tool, minor.

confirmed_findings.title: docs/mixing_efficiency.md implies all derived estimates get both the N2 and dT/dz masks, but K_T is never masked on N2 and K_rho is never masked on dT/dz
confirmed_findings.claim: docs/mixing_efficiency.md:58-61 states collectively that 'Estimates are masked (NaN) where N^2 < 1e-9 s^-2 ... or |dT/dz| < 1e-4 K/m', leading a reader to conclude K_T is NaN in unstratified water and K_rho is NaN in well-mixed water, whereas the code masks per-variable: K_T only on dTdz+chi (mixing.py:504), K_rho only on N2+epsilon (:510), only Gamma on both.
confirmed_findings.evidence: docs/mixing_efficiency.md:58-61 vs mixing.py:498-510: grad_ok gates K_T/Gamma; strat_ok gates Gamma/K_rho. The function docstring (:454-464) documents per-variable masking correctly; only the .md generalizes. Confirmed behaviorally: with N2=1e-5 and dTdz=1e-4 at the floor, K_rho stayed finite; tests/test_processing_mixing.py:208-209 asserts 'K_rho does not depend on dT/dz'.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: Read docs/mixing_efficiency.md:56-61 and mixing.py:498-510; optionally run mixing_coefficients(1e-9,1e-9,1e-10,0.01) — K_T finite despite N2 below floor while Gamma/K_rho NaN.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite the sentence per-variable mirroring mixing.py:454-464 (Gamma both floors; K_T dT/dz only; K_rho N2 plus the ceiling).

verdict.reasoning: Confirmed by running code. docs/mixing_efficiency.md:58-61 collectively states 'Estimates are masked (NaN) where N^2<1e-9 ... or |dT/dz|<1e-4', implying all of K_T/Gamma/K_rho get both masks. Code masks per-variable: mixing.py:504 K_T on grad_ok&chi_ok only (no N2); :510 K_rho on strat_ok&eps_ok only (no dTdz); :505 Gamma on both. Reproduced: mixing_coefficients(N2=1e-10,dTdz=0.01) -> K_T=5.0e-5 finite while Gamma/K_rho NaN; (N2=1e-5,dTdz=1e-6) -> K_rho=2.0e-4 finite while K_T/Gamma NaN. tests/test_processing_mixing.py:208-209 asserts K_rho independent of dT/dz. Function docstring (mixing.py:454-464) is per-variable correct; only the .md generalizes. Touches validity/masking of published K_T/K_rho. Not a prior duplicate (#30 was the K_rho ceiling).

confirmed_findings.title: L1 GRADT uses the legacy first-difference method, diverging from the ODAS default high-pass gradient (corr 0.988 on real data) with no documentation
confirmed_findings.claim: p_to_L1 computes the published ATOMIX GRADT variable as fs*diff(T)/speed (ODAS's legacy 'first_difference' option) while the ODAS reference default is the 'high_pass' method on the pre-emphasized signal, so the exported L1 GRADT differs sample-by-sample from the reference product and the deviation is documented nowhere.
confirmed_findings.evidence: convert.py:517-523 (diff, last sample filled with dTdt[-1] instead of ODAS's 0 — a second small deviation) vs odas/odas_p2mat.m:232 (default_gradT_method = 'high_pass') and odas/make_gradT_odas.m:219 (high-pass) / :258-259 (legacy). Measured on 0002.p vs MATLAB gradT1 in _allch.nc: correlation 0.988, max abs diff 149.6 K/m against rms 1.89 K/m (differences at high frequency/low speed, the sinc response of a first difference). No in-package consumer reads GRADT back (chi computes its own gradients); grep for 'high_pass|first_difference|gradT_method' in src/ and docs/ returns nothing.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: PYTHONPATH=... python3: compute g=append(diff(T1_dT1)*fs, last)/speed_fast for 0002.p and compare to _allch.nc gradT1 -> corr ~0.988, maxabs ~150. Compare odas/make_gradT_odas.m and odas_p2mat.m:232.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Port the high-pass method for parity, or document the legacy first-difference choice in the GRADT attrs ('method: first_difference') and fill the final sample with 0 to match ODAS.

verdict.reasoning: convert.py:521-523 computes GRADT=fs*diff(T)/speed on the reconstructed fast temperature (verified pf.channels['T1_dT1'] returns degC, mean 26.06, on 0002.p). ODAS make_gradT_odas.m:60 default_method='high_pass' and odas_p2mat.m:232 default_gradT_method='high_pass'; first_difference is the legacy option. grep confirms 'first_difference|high_pass|gradT_method' absent from src/ and docs/ -> undocumented; no internal consumer (chi builds its own gradient spectrum). Core claim (undocumented non-default method, HF divergence) stands. HOWEVER the 'second deviation' sub-claim is FALSE: ODAS first_difference does gradT(end)=gradT(end-1), overwriting the appended 0, so convert.py's last-sample=dTdt[-1] MATCHES ODAS exactly. Did not numerically reproduce the 0.988 corr, but divergence is certain from the sinc response of a first difference. Export-only product; nit.

confirmed_findings.title: chi_mathematics.md Method-1 Step 7 says chi is the grid argmin; code applies a parabolic log-space refinement shifting the reported chi up to ~2.3%
confirmed_findings.claim: Section 6 Step 7 (lines 343-349) documents 'chi = argmin_chi cost(chi)' over the 200-point grid as the reported value, but _chi_from_epsilon refines the minimum by parabolic interpolation in log10(chi) (chi.py:283-296), moving the reported chi by up to half a grid step (~2.3%) relative to the documented pure argmin.
confirmed_findings.evidence: chi.py:283-296 ('Parabolic refinement ... the grid is ~4.7% coarse (200 points over 4 decades)'; |dx| <= h guaranteed) vs docs/chi_mathematics.md:343-349. h = 4/199 = 0.0201 decades; half-step shift = 10^0.0101 - 1 = 2.3%.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: Read chi.py:280-298 and docs/chi_mathematics.md:331-349 (Step 6 gives the exact 200-point/4-decade grid, so the omission is unambiguous).
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Append to Step 7: the grid minimum is refined by a parabola through the minimum and its two neighbours in (log10 chi, cost) space, removing the ~4.7% quantization.

verdict.reasoning: chi.py:283-296 refines the grid argmin by parabolic interpolation in log10(chi): dx=0.5*h*(y0-y2)/denom. docs/chi_mathematics.md:343-347 Step 7 documents only 'Report the grid-minimising chi = argmin_chi cost(chi)'. grep of chi_mathematics.md shows the only refine/parabola mentions (lines 380,423,447) are in the Method-2/MLE section, not Method-1 Step 7. Grid: 200 pts over 4 decades -> h=4/199=0.0201 dec; half-step shift 10^0.0101-1 ~= 2.3%. Genuine doc-code inconsistency on the reported (published) chi value; the code is the correct/improved version, the doc is incomplete. Nit.

confirmed_findings.title: Nasmyth universal-spectrum 'formula/coefficient' tests are tautological: expected values recomputed from the same inline constants as the implementation, providing zero independent validation of the published Lueck fit
confirmed_findings.claim: test_formula_values (tests/test_scor160_nasmyth.py:27-31) and test_lueck_coefficients (tests/test_epsilon.py:126-134) compute 'expected' as 8.05*x**(1/3)/(1+(20.6*x)**3.715) — character-identical to the implementation at scor160/nasmyth.py:72 — so a transcription error in the published coefficients would be faithfully mirrored in both test and code and never caught.
confirmed_findings.evidence: nasmyth.py:72, test_scor160_nasmyth.py:30, test_epsilon.py:133 — three identical expressions; assert_allclose then compares the function to itself. (Constants are correct against the Lueck improved fit per ODAS nasmyth.m, so no present-day numeric impact.) The integral test (rel=0.15) and X_95/LUECK_A pins are the only semi-independent checks.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: diff the expressions at the three cited locations.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Pin literal numeric G2 values from the published tabulation (Lueck TN-028 / McMillan et al. 2016) with a citation instead of re-deriving from the same expression.

verdict.reasoning: Verified by inspection: nasmyth.py:72 returns 8.05*x**(1/3)/(1+(20.6*x)**3.715); test_scor160_nasmyth.py:30 and test_epsilon.py:133 recompute the identical expression then assert_allclose -> tautological, cannot catch a coefficient transcription error (a copy-paste change would propagate to both). Constants are correct vs the Lueck improved fit, so zero present-day numeric impact. Other tests (integral rtol=0.15, X_95, LUECK_A pins) provide partial independent validation. Narrow, real test-quality nit touching formula validation; scientifically inconsequential.

confirmed_findings.title: Binned/combo epsilonLnSigma-chiLnSigma lose the 'NOT the sigma of the combined mean' caveat, inviting misuse as an error bar on the binned mean (overstating uncertainty by ~sqrt(n))
confirmed_findings.claim: Per-profile epsilonLnSigma carries an explicit attrs comment that it is the expected single-probe sigma_ln with 'no sqrt(n) reduction applied' (epsilon_combine.py:180-184), but binning strips all per-variable attrs (perturb/binning.py:503-505) and the combo schema re-attaches only units=''/long_name='sigma of ln(epsilon)' (netcdf_schema.py:233-237), so in the published product the RMS-binned sigma is indistinguishable from an uncertainty on the binned epsilonMean, which it overstates by ~sqrt(n_windows*n_probes) (~3x for n~10).
confirmed_findings.evidence: epsilon_combine.py:174-186 (caveat comment); perturb/binning.py:307-317 bins LnSigma as sqrt(mean(sigma^2)) (RMS, single-window semantics) and :503-505 emits vars with no attrs; netcdf_schema.py:233-237 restores no comment field.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: Read the three cited spans; or run the perturb bin stage on a diss per-profile NC and ncdump -h: epsilonLnSigma has no comment attribute while the input file does.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Add the caveat (and the RMS-of-per-window-sigma semantics after binning) to the epsilonLnSigma/chiLnSigma schema entries.

verdict.reasoning: processing/epsilon_combine.py:180-184 (mk_epsilon_mean, used by perturb pipeline.py:1045) attaches comment 'Expected single-probe sigma_ln(epsilon)... NOT the sigma of the combined epsilonMean (no sqrt(n) reduction applied)'. binning.py:503-505 rebuilds vars as bare (['bin','profile'],arr) with NO attrs, dropping the comment; apply_schema (netcdf_schema.py:470-472) then adds only COMBO_SCHEMA long_name/units (:233-237, no comment). LnSigma binned as sqrt(mean(sigma^2)) (:314-317, :613-616), single-window RMS semantics. So the binned/combo epsilonLnSigma loses the caveat. Mitigation: long_name 'sigma of ln(epsilon)' is still technically accurate (a spread, not a SEM), so misuse-as-error-bar risk is bounded -> nit, not a wrong published number.

confirmed_findings.title: pyturb eps output S_sh* is labeled 'cleaned shear spectrum' even though Goodman cleaning is off by default, in which case it is the raw spectrum
confirmed_findings.claim: rename_eps_dataset writes l3.sh_spec_clean with long_name 'cleaned shear spectrum (probe i)' (_compat.py:169-174), but with pyturb-cli's default --goodman off (cli.py:73-74) process_l3 sets sh_spec_clean to a copy of the raw spectrum (scor160/l3.py:167-168), so the default-path output variable is mislabeled as noise-cleaned when no coherent-noise removal was applied.
confirmed_findings.evidence: _compat.py:170-174 attaches the long_name unconditionally; scor160/l3.py:155-168 'else: sh_freq_spec_clean = sh_freq_spec.copy()' when no vib windows; eps.py:74 threads goodman=args.goodman defaulting False (cli.py:73).

confirmed_findings.title: perturb silently uses vertical |dP/dt| speed for glide vehicles at the default speed.method='pressure' (no warning) -> ~5x epsilon on real Slocum glider data
confirmed_findings.claim: For a glide-direction vehicle (e.g. slocum_glider MicroRider) processed with the default speed.method='pressure', compute_speed_for_pfile returns vertical |dP/dt| as the through-water speed with no warning, ~32% below the true along-path speed; because epsilon ~ U^(-4.3) this inflates published epsilon by ~5x, and the sibling rsi path (helpers.prepare_profiles) warns about exactly this case while the perturb path does not.
confirmed_findings.evidence: speed.py:91-96 the method=='pressure' branch calls compute_speed_fast (|dP/dt|) and returns, never checking vehicle direction; compute_speed_for_pfile takes `vehicle` only to resolve tau (speed.py:80) and issues no glide warning. The perturb default is method 'pressure' (perturb/config.py:149 runtime dict and :649 template). This is the speed injected by the perturb pipeline (pipeline.py:1073-1081). By contrast the rsi path warns 'Vehicle direction glide but speed is being computed from |dP/dt| ... epsilon scales as ~U^4 and will be strongly biased' (helpers.py:304-307), but perturb precomputes speed_fast via compute_speed_for_pfile so prepare_profiles takes the precomputed branch (helpers.py:292-293) and never reaches its own warning. Measured on real Slocum MR file /Users/pat/tpw/turbulence/MR/AIOP2_SL685_0450.p over the glide segment (|pitch|>25 deg): default pressure |dP/dt| median = 0.2922 m/s vs EM-flowmeter through-water truth 0.4296 m/s = 32.0% underestimate; compute_speed_for_pfile emitted ZERO warnings. Code's own measured leverage eps~U^-4.3 (see how_to_verify) gives (0.680)^-4.3 = 5.25x epsilon inflation. ARCTERX VMP in-house data is unaffected (VMP vertical speed = through-water speed).

Separately confirm eps~U^-4.3 by calling odas_tpw.rsi.dissipation._compute_epsilon on a VMP .p with speed=0.96 vs 1.44 and taking log(ratio)/log(1.5) (=-4.25). Contrast helpers.py:303-309 (rsi warns) with speed.py:91-96 (perturb path does not).

confirmed_findings.title: ARCTERX epsilon.salinity comment claims CTD-derived salinity, but the code uses fixed S=35 for the viscosity that scales published epsilon
confirmed_findings.claim: The ARCTERX campaign config annotates `epsilon.salinity: null` as "use CTD-derived salinity (preferred)", but null resolves to a fixed S=35 PSU (visc35) for the epsilon kinematic-viscosity used in every published epsilon window; perturb has no CTD/measured-salinity code path for epsilon at all (only chi does).
confirmed_findings.evidence: ARCTERX/perturb.yaml:84 comment vs reality: merge_config drops None (src/odas_tpw/config_base.py:230 `return {k: v ... if v is not None}`), so the resolved epsilon cfg has no `salinity` key (verified: keys = ['T1_norm','T2_norm','despike_smooth','despike_thresh','diagnostics','epsilon_minimum','f_AA','fft_length','fit_order','goodman']). `_compute_epsilon` is then called with salinity=None (default, src/odas_tpw/rsi/dissipation.py:86) -> prepare_profiles sets sal_fast=None (src/odas_tpw/rsi/helpers.py:347-348) -> dissipation.py:232-233 `nu_out = visc35(temp_win)` = fixed S=35. This directly contradicts the package docs (docs/perturb/configuration.md:161 "null = S=35 approx") and the default template (src/odas_tpw/perturb/config.py:597 "null = 35, fixed S"). Only chi exposes a "measured" option (configuration.md:187; pipeline.py:1438-1477). Measured numeric impact for ARCTERX (T~28C): visc(28,34.5)=8.7752e-7 vs visc(28,35)=8.7838e-7 => -0.099%, so published epsilon differs from the claimed in-situ-salinity value by ~0.1% (epsilon is ~linear in nu through the resolved-variance/spectral integral). Worst realistic case for other campaigns (Arctic S=32 @0C / hypersaline S=40 @20C) is ~1.0-1.3%.

confirmed_findings.title: GPS positions beyond the fix record are always linearly extrapolated (never NaN), and the only guardrail warning is silently suppressed when the record's extreme times carry NaN lat/lon
confirmed_findings.claim: `_warn_outside_coverage` only warns (it never gates positions to NaN), and its coverage window `[t_min,t_max]` is taken from ALL decoded times (gps.py:319-320 `float(np.nanmin(t))/np.nanmax(t)`), whereas the interpolator's real domain is the finite-(time,lat) node span (`_finite_interp1d`, gps.py:57-75, drops non-finite nodes). When lat/lon are NaN at the record's temporal extremes (a GPS fix dropout at the start/end of the file), queries that fall between the last finite fix and t_max are linearly extrapolated with NO warning, producing fabricated positions that feed per-profile lat/lon coordinates, the geospatial_lat/lon_min/max attributes (combo.py:170-177), and the TEOS-10 SA conversion.
confirmed_findings.evidence: Reproduced: gps_gap.nc with t in [0,1000] but lat/lon set NaN for t>800. Provider reports _t_min,_t_max = 0.0,1000.0 (from the full time array), while the interpolator is built only over finite nodes t<=800. Queries at t=900/950/1000 return extrapolated values with warned=False in all three cases (warnings.catch_warnings shows no 'outside the GPS record coverage' warning). The extrapolation used the slope of the last two finite fixes; for a curving ship track the extrapolated position over a 200 s dropout can be off by hundreds of metres, and the documented 60 s tolerance warning never fires.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: Build a NetCDF with time 0..1000 (units 'seconds since 1970-01-01'), lat=15+t*1e-4, lon=145+t*1e-4, then set lat[t>800]=lon[t>800]=NaN. Construct GPSFromNetCDF(...,max_time_diff=60); query g.lat([950.0]) inside warnings.catch_warnings(record=True): the value is finite (extrapolated past the last fix at t=800) and no coverage warning is recorded, because g._t_max=1000 >= 950.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Compute _t_min/_t_max in _finite_interp1d from the finite-node span actually used by the interpolator (the good-mask min/max), and pass those to _warn_outside_coverage; optionally offer a config to NaN (rather than extrapolate) positions beyond t_max+max_time_diff so fabricated fixes cannot silently enter the geospatial attrs and SA.

confirmed_findings.title: speed.py docstring inverts the dbar<->metre conversion and the sign of the systematic speed bias
confirmed_findings.claim: The docstring states the pressure-method speed is '|dP/dt| in dbar/s treated as m/s ... 1 dbar ~ 1.01-1.02 m ... The ~1-2 % systematic speed bias', but 1 dbar is ~0.99 m (not 1.01-1.02 m), so dP/dt in dbar/s slightly OVER-states the true m/s speed (opposite sign to what the docstring implies) and the true magnitude is <1%, not 1-2%.
confirmed_findings.evidence: speed.py:65-68. gsw.z_from_p(p,lat=15) gives depth/p = 0.9941 (p=10) down to 0.9930 (p=500) m per dbar; the instantaneous rate factor dz/dP = 1/(rho*g*1e-4) is ~0.991 m/dbar (deep) to ~1.000 m/dbar (warm surface). In every case 1 dbar <= ~1.00 m, never 1.01-1.02 m; the docstring's number is the inverse relation (1 m ~ 1.006-1.009 dbar). Consequently dP/dt over-states the true fall speed by ~0.0-0.9%, so with epsilon ~ U^(-4.3) (measured in this code) epsilon is UNDER-stated by ~0-4%; the docstring's inverted factor points a reader to the wrong bias sign. Magnitude '1-2 %' is ~2x the real <1%.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: PYTHONPATH=/Users/pat/tpw/tpw/turbulence/src python3 -c 'import gsw; print([-gsw.z_from_p(p,15)/p for p in (10,50,100,200,500)])' -> all ~0.993-0.994 (m per dbar), never 1.01-1.02. Read speed.py:64-69.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Correct to '1 dbar ~ 0.99 m (equivalently 1 m ~ 1.006-1.009 dbar) so |dP/dt| slightly over-states the true m/s speed by <1%'; adjust the '1-2 %' figure to '<1%' and note the resulting epsilon bias is a slight under-estimate given the negative U-leverage.

confirmed_findings.title: _calc_lag in FP07 in-situ calibration lacks the non-finite/flatline guard its sibling ct_align has: a single NaN or constant reference/FP07 segment silently returns lag = -max_lag_seconds (-10 s), poisoning the median lag that aligns the Steinhart-Hart fit (worst case ~9% chi, ~0.5 C absolute-T error)
confirmed_findings.claim: fp07_cal._calc_lag does not skip a profile segment whose cross-correlation normalizer is non-finite or zero (NaN sample or flatlined signal); instead it leaves the correlation un-normalized (or NaN) and argmax(|corr|) over the negative-lag window returns index 0, i.e. the most-negative searched lag (-max_lag_seconds = -10 s). That poisoned per-profile lag enters lags_list unfiltered and can flip the median lag used to align the reference for the Steinhart-Hart temperature-scale fit that feeds all chi.
confirmed_findings.evidence: fp07_cal.py:135 `if norm > 0: corr = corr / norm` has no else/continue, and lines 149-156 (must_be_negative branch) do `idx = int(np.argmax(np.abs(corr_search))); return lags_search[idx]/fs` so an all-zero (flatline, norm==0) or NaN (norm non-finite) correlation returns lags_search[0] = the most-negative lag. Lines 261-267 append the returned lag to lags_list with no NaN/outlier filtering and take np.median. The sibling ct_align.py:121-126 explicitly guards this exact case (`if not np.isfinite(norm) or norm <= 0: continue`, with comment 'argmax(|corr|) of an all-zero correlation pick[s] index 0, i.e. the most-negative lag ... a maximally-wrong estimate') and ct_align.py:110-114 additionally interpolates isolated non-finite samples; fp07_cal._calc_lag has neither. Measured on real cast SN479_0010: _calc_lag on a flatlined segment returns -10.0 s (corr 0.0); with one NaN injected into the reference or FP07 segment returns -10.0 s (corr NaN); the clean lag is -0.109 s. Refitting Steinhart-Hart with a poisoned median lag of -10 s vs the correct -0.109 s changes the order-2 coefficients from [3.449e-3, 3.251e-4, 3.517e-6] to [3.447e-3, 2.248e-4, -2.196e-4], producing chi(poisoned)/chi(correct)=1.0915 (+9.15%) and a 0.47 C absolute-T shift on a mid-cast profile. Latent on the default path: convert_jac_t (channels.py:245-254) is a polynomial and never emits NaN (only convert_jac_c does, via v_part==0, channels.py:238); a scan of all 29 in-house casts found 0 poisoned profiles across 464 profiles, so the default JAC_T pipeline is unaffected. It becomes live for any configured reference that can drop out (hotel-merged SBE/glider references) or if FP07 deconvolution ever yields a non-finite sample.
confirmed_findings.how_to_verify: cd /Users/pat/tpw/tpw/turbulence; run: PYTHONPATH=/Users/pat/tpw/tpw/turbulence/src python3 -c "import numpy as np; from odas_tpw.perturb import fp07_cal as F; print(F._calc_lag(np.full(2000,22.0), np.full(2000,1000.0), 64.0, must_be_negative=True)); t=22+0.5*np.sin(np.linspace(0,20,2000)); f=1000-30*np.sin(np.linspace(0,20,2000)-0.1); tn=t.copy(); tn[1000]=np.nan; print('clean',F._calc_lag(t,f,64.0)); print('one NaN in ref',F._calc_lag(tn,f,64.0))" -> observe the flat case returns (-10.0, 0.0) and the NaN case returns (-10.0, nan) while the clean case is near 0; contrast ct_align.py:121-126 which continues past this case. Then propagate: refit with shift_edge_hold(JAC_T, round(-10*fs)) vs round(-0.109*fs) and compare mean(diff(fast T1_dT1)^2) over a profile (script in scratchpad) -> ~1.09x chi ratio.
confirmed_findings.suggested_fix: Mirror ct_align: in _calc_lag, after computing norm, `if not np.isfinite(norm) or norm <= 0: return np.nan, np.nan` (and have the caller skip NaN lags), and/or interpolate isolated non-finite samples before diff/lfilter as ct_align._repair_nonfinite does; additionally drop non-finite entries from lags_list before np.median at fp07_cal.py:267.
